item.title: I missed it... yesterday night generational wealth slipped through my grasp. I'm only comfortably typing about it and no… — 9 replies — 36.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
I missed it... yesterday night generational wealth slipped through my grasp. I'm only comfortably typing about it and no…

Open thread • Replies: 9 • Updated: 2026-03-16 10:31 UTC

OP

I missed it... yesterday night generational wealth slipped through my grasp. I'm only comfortably typing about it and not from a ditch with a shotgun barrel pressed against my tonsils is because i was rlly drunk yesterday.

I was drunk AND high. Really bored, 2am on a friday. I'm usually a highroller but i'm way reformed so i decided to drop in a cool ez breezy 50 dollar deposit which is a light years difference compared to my usual heft (in the thousands. but that was decades ago). I played around a bit. Never breaking above $120 and never falling below $40. Its the online game so i am i guess at the mercy of RNG rig as there is no live dealer but i really have a soft spot for an occasional game session of very low value. But at the end at about 4 am i was getting bored. No big wins no big losses.

I calmly withdrew my 50 and decide to play with my newly gained 20. Again, this is far from how i used to be. So i'm drunker and higher as the night progresses and i start hitting a flow state. I switch to 1 dollar bets as i enter the digital backrooms in my mind, testing my theory about how the rig is implemented. I start STANDING on literal 5's and 8's. I start doubling 17's, i start splitting any and every doubles. I play like an absolute moron but the games rig machine is in absolute shock and awe.

I have in my betting history: 33 one dollar blackjack wins in a row. In the back of my head after winning about 20 in a row i think "yeah, this thing wants me to deposit a 10,000 bomb and play it immediately just to start a loss streak". To my absolute and utter bewilderment i continue to win, and win. Still playing like an absolute system breaking hacker. The dealer once showed a 5 and i had a K-9 and i hit. I pull an Ace to make me 20 and low and behold the dealer was going to grab the ace for the win.

I would have played those 33 games with over $1,000 bets many years ago. I used to have balls, i tell you. Balls.

Early replies (first 3)

61985062
>>61985037
When you pay with pennies they let you win often and repeatedly, no harm for them. You certainly didn't miss "generational wealth" here.

61985063
The house always wins. Even if you'd made these bets at 1k a pop you'd still be down overall. And more importantly, you'd have just lost it later.
Because gamblers aren't happy until they lose. And that's why you feel like shit right now.

61985225
>>61985062
This it’s probably set that way purpose in order to get people to then deposit large sums and then lose it all.

Just be glad you didn’t fall for it.

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

61985245
>>61985063
if i won the otherwise millions of dollars by winning 33 hands in a row i'm sure i would have maybe put something in like "25,000 to play with" but even then, imagine. I think even a real gambling gambler will finally stop once they hit the jackpot.

>>61985225
I really really really wonder if it would have cut my streak if i wasn't on dollar bets. I even started doing Dime bets during the win streak, still wins.

61985295
never understood playing casino games

might as well burn your money

61985632
>>61985037
top tier copypasta. I know someone will read this and relapse.

61985733
>>61985037
So you were nowhere near close generational wealth and only won because the rigged machine let you win with tiny bets. This is enough to make you want to kill yourself? Jesus dude you're hanging on a thread out there

61986871
>>61985037
You would have gotten different cards if you were playing big money. It's rigged. You can't think you'd be rich if you'd bet big because you wouldn't have gotten those hands, and the system would have fleeced you. You should actually be glad you didn't get suckered into betting big, because that's what the system was trying to convince you to do, before it wiped you out.

61990317
>>61985245
>if i won the otherwise millions of dollars by winning 33 hands in a row
you must be retarded to think there is any chance they let that happen.

>"yeah, this thing wants me to deposit a 10,000 bomb and play it immediately just to start a loss streak"
this is the 100% realistic outcome

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

61975253

nah not wrapped if you take the time to look it up. it stays native and can actually be put to work instead of just sitting there

61975277
>>61967738
Anon, that’s literally the point. Bitcoin’s value isn’t backed by a company or government, it’s backed by people agreeing it’s scarce and useful. Digital receipts don’t matter, adoption and network effect do.

61977174
>>61967782
I guess you will stick to sending remittances lol

61977180
>>61967712
give me all 21 million of them

61977934

ngmi

61978229

>>61964560
Anything can be a store of value if we manage to organize a secure and efficient system of exchange. Yes gold is in principle as arbitrary as Bitcoin, but what people don't realize is just how difficult it is to come up with a practical medium of exchange. You need something that is scarce, secure, liquid and stable, because yeah obviously you just want something that just acts as a reference for value and nothing more.The "issue" with modern currencies that lolberts and schizos usually complain about is the idea of having centralized entities in charge of creating and regulating the medium, i.e. central banks. Bitcoin was supposedly an alternative under the delusional assumption that people were going to adopt it massively because muh banks are le evil. Now this is the crazy thing, suppose we do come together and say well lets adopt crypto universally as a system, the stuff with the transfer times and security is not even a big issue if enough people participate ( instead of what we spend on current currency infrastructure which still costs we spend it on crypto infrastructure in some way so enough people are mining all the time), the thing is why the fuck would people agree to use Bitcoin if already most of it is hoarded by a handful of people? We are by fiat allowing these hoarders to control the economy even though we are perfectly capable of creating a new crypto network? Just because it was first even if the code and idea is perfectly reproducibly? Except we are painfully retarded (which we are lol), there is no fucking reason to adopt Bitcoin. It's literally as retarded as someone buying gold at a stupid price hoarding it and expecting people to still buy it at that price because eventually we will adopt gold as THE medium of exchange.

You’re doing the right thing anon. Curious to see how TBVs scale once live.

And no, their cake didn't come from some abstract financial mechanism that was just a Ponzi scheme like crypto is. If you bought all the gold, silver and copper in the world, the modern day tech simply could not be possible. The world was in the stone age or whatever before precious metals and technology. Prior to Bitcoin the world was working perfectly fine. If you had an the gold, silver and copper in the world, people would need that shit for modern technology, for electricity, for computers, if you have all the bitchcoin in the world, no one would even know or care, it doesn't affect anyone or anything because it's not even a real world commodity. It does nothing that can't be done better than gold, silver and copper, and it does nothing better than a crypto that was actually backed by Gold, silver and copper which places world limits on the currency itself because it's backed by something tangible.

Can't believe it still has to be spelled out for idiots, but if you read this and you still pretend that crypto is but a crypto scam where gamblers win at the expense of the majority, then yeah, you deserve crypto and I hope you put all your earnings into that.

61990093
Imagine buying an apartment in a bank owned building.
It's easier to live there because the owner takes care of everything. You dont have to care about maintaining, cleaning, dealing with electrcity providers, water suppliers... everything is easy.
The catch is that the owner has the keys and can change the lock when he wants to. And every time you want to enter or leave your apartment you have to ask the owner if he's fine with it (btw, the owner doesnt like to be bothered during weekends)

On the other hand, you could buy your own apartment and have to struggle with dealing with eveything. Cleaning, suppliers, sevice providers... everything. But nobody can kick you out or change locks. You can do whatever you want, even on weekends.

Now you understand Bitcoin.

You won't understand that analogy because you retards are in a cult, it's just going to be "waaahhh you can't eat gold either" completely ignoring the fact that gold and silver are used in real world technology and you can't have the modern tech without the actual real physical commodity, and one of the reasons why gold, silver and copper are money, is that it was money, they were already in demand high value commodities and the demand was and still is always constant, which means there is always a buyer in the future for your precious metals regardless of confidence because people want electricity, medical technology and whatever else those commodities provide. That buyer always available meant that anyone who wantes to store value for the future, could buy it, hold it and sell it later down the line and there would always be a floor price to it because of the demand whether high or low. Gold, silver and copper could never go down to zero because they're not based on confidence, they're based on real world demand. If you buy up all the gold and silver, someone will be there to buy it from you and the real world modern tech will collapse without it, but crypto? Doesn't change anything, you can have all the bitcoin in the world and it doesn't stop anything and no one would care.

A lot of it is just ownership in private companies, mostly mid sized, but some of them quite small, like a local HVAC or plumbing company.
The problem is, it's hard to value these companies if the metric is something other than straight profitability. How do analysts properly value hundreds or even thousands of private companies? In a downturn, these companies look artificially more stable than public companies, because they don't see hourly fluctuations, but the downside is that a company can be silently failing, and then one day it just gets written down 80% out of the blue.

item.content:encoded:
I thought oracles were tough to make/maintain?

Open thread • Replies: 24 • Updated: 2026-03-16 05:17 UTC

OP

I thought oracles were tough to make/maintain? Why didn't Hyperliquid bend the knee to Chainlink? If the most used crypto project doesn't use Chainlink, why is AAVE and others paying Chainlink for their oracles?

Early replies (first 3)

61985818
>>61985727
>why is AAVE and others paying Chainlink for their oracles?
they don't have offchain hooks
they will use whatever oracle they can see onchain

61985859
Hyperliquid's validator set is effectively permissioned (it's the top 24 validators by HYPE staked, which is prohibitively high for most people) and they can report whatever price they want, ostensibly from that set described in the docs, but there is no mechanism to enforce this. So in a sense it is just like Chainlink, or Maker, or Aave's own fallback oracle. Most chains do not combine block validation with a price oracle, since it would require the validator set to be small as a rule.

61986446
>>61985727
This is becoming a lot more common. Or native oracles. Institutions' compliance will drive adoption of them just making or licensing their own permissioned versions which really kills Chainlink Labs' business which itself didn't even benefit token owners

no refunds you stupid nigger, go push the boulder uphill some more kek

61987499
>>61985727
>f the most used crypto project doesn't use Chainlink, why is AAVE and others paying Chainlink for their oracles?
Hyperliquid has two major components: HyperCore and HyperEVM.

Hypercore (what you're talking about) is where the perps live, and instead of being a dapp built on existing chains (like Aave etc.) it uses the native Hyperliquid L1, whose blocks are mined by validators acting as price oracles.
Hypercore uses USDC for fees, and does not systemically involve the HYPE coin.
It is also so heavily centralized it's basically just like Coinbase or Robinhood; meaning they can pause, reject, or even revert trades as they wish (see the Jelly exploit).

Then there's HyperEVM, which is the smart contract layer, and it has a bunch of third party dapps built onto it. The HYPE coin is used here for gas. These dapps tend to use Chainlink.

61987524
The native perps on HL use their own oracle yes.
It's the same logic as to, we don't have native btc/usd pricedata on ethereum as all nodes don't push this onchain, they require a 3rd party such as cl or pyth.
However, for custom deployed perps, these will use 3rd party oracles. Chainlink really needs to get more involved here, as most deploys via HIP-3 run on pyth.

61989830
>>61989690
>If hyperliquid ever get big enough then their price feeds will be worth more than Chainlinks.
That doesn't make any sense. Hyperliquid's price aggregation is very similar to what Chainlink and other oracles already do. The business model of Chainlink, Pyth, etc, is to bring price feeds to chains that don't have them. Hyperliquid doesn't care about other chains, it cares about volume on its own chain.
